data.title: NewLake Capital Partners: Cannabis Meltdown Continues - Rich Dividends For The Opportunistic
data.description:
NLCP Remains Compelling As A Dividend Story, Assuming No Further Tenant Issues

We previously covered NewLake Capital Partners (OTCQX:NLCP) in March 2025, discussing why the sell-off had triggered an excellent buying opportunity for those seeking rich return profiles, attributed to the overly discounted valuations.

Combined with the higher upside potential from current levels, significantly made sweeter by the highly compelling forward dividend yields of 11.51% and the healthy balance sheet with low debt, we had rated the stock as a Strong Buy then.

NLCP 1Y Stock Price

Since then, NLCP has already further declined by -4.1%, with a similar underperformance also observed in its cannabis REIT peers.

Much of their headwinds are attributed to their numerous tenant default issues, along with the pessimism observed in the wider legal cannabis market and the reduced likelihood of a cannabis federal legalization during the new US administration over the next four years.

For reference, licensed cannabis operators face intense competition from illicit market players, as some New York dispensaries also sell "from unauthorized sources or is grown out of state, an illegal practice that has been called the industry’s open secret."

These developments have naturally contributed to the legal cannabis operators' impacted performance, attributed to their razor-thin profit margins in a challenging macroeconomic environment, worsened by the industry's lack of legal banking access.

Combined with the impacted consumer discretionary spending, it is unsurprising that NLCP's peer, Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR), has already reported numerous rental collection issues, as four of its tenants default on their rents by March 2025 with 27.8% of its revenues at risk.

These issues have already contributed to the REIT's impacted FQ4'24 and FQ1'25 performance, as observed in the negative rental revenues and AFFO per share growth on a YoY basis.

The same tenant issue has been reported by NLCP, as Revolutionary Clinics and Calypso Enterprises report operating challenges from H2'24 and fail to pay rent as per contract.

With these two tenants comprising 12.8% (-0.7 points YoY) of the REIT's FY2024 annualized rental revenues and 13.5% of its FY2023 revenues, it is unsurprising that the market has discounted its prospects, since more tenant defaults may occur ahead.

IIPR's & NLCP's Tenant List

Even so, while both IIPR and NLCP may share a somewhat similar tenant list as shown above, with red indicating troubled tenants with recent rental defaults and green indicating Multi-State Operators with stable financial metrics, it is apparent that NLCP has had a drastically smaller exposure to those with troubled cash flows.

This implies that NLCP may be somewhat better positioned than IIPR, since the former continues to report a relatively stable FQ1'25 rental incomes of $12.58M (+2.5% QoQ/+3.7% YoY) and AFFO per share of $0.51 (-1.9% QoQ/-1.9% YoY), aided by the 98% of rental collection.

While NLCP may have a very limited number of tenants, with the top five tenants already comprising 62.4% of its FY2024 annualized rental revenues (+5.3 points YoY), readers may want to note that most of these tenants continue to report decent top/bottom-lines in their recent earnings call.

For example, Curaleaf (CURA:CA) (OTCPK:CURLF), which commands 22.8% of NLCP's revenues, has reported stable performance metrics in FQ4'24 while consistently growing its footprints and expanding its product lines into hemp derived THC seltzers.

The similarly promising developments have also been reported by Cresco Labs (CSE:CL) (OTCQX:CRLBF) (FSE:6CQ) and Trulieve Cannabis Corp. (TRUL) (TCNNF), aside from Cannabist, which is undergoing footprint/operation optimization and C3 Industries, not publicly listed.

These imply that IIPR ongoing tenants' headwinds may not be directly translated to all legal cannabis operators, with NLCP's recent stock price correction merely attributed to an instance of when "a falling tide sinking all ships."

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why the consensus forward estimates remain somewhat optimistic, with NLCP expected to report a somewhat stable top/bottom-line performance over the next two years.

Assuming no further tenant troubles, the cannabis REIT may be able to continue paying out rich dividends as well, despite the increasingly elevated FQ1'25 AFFO payout ratio at 84% (+1 points QoQ/+5.7% YoY).

NLCP Valuations

These reasons are also why NLCP remains compelling at FWD Price/AFFO valuations of 7.54x, down from the 1Y mean of 9.28x and 4Y mean of 9.91x.

The same discount may also be observed in IIPR at FWD Price/AFFO valuations of 7.36x, compared to its 1Y mean of 10.92x and 5Y mean of 14.97x, as the market's pessimism surrounding the prospects of the cannabis operators and cannabis REIT markets in general triggers opportunistic entry points for value oriented investors.

Risk Warning

Nonetheless, it goes without saying that investing here carries notable risks, aside from the challenging market condition.

This is especially since the new US administration's intensified tariff war may trigger a higher cost of production, given the cannabis industry's reliance on a global supply chain for "essential equipment to grow cannabis, machinery, and accessories—everything from vaporizers to packaging and cartridges," including China and Southeast Asian countries.

For now, Justin Tacy, the Vice President of Marketing at PAX, a cannabis brand selling both cannabis products and vaporizers, has already highlighted that "high cannabis taxes, combined with tariffs, raise costs and may push buyers to the illicit market or force producers to cut costs, potentially compromising quality."

This is worsened by the potentially driven "up prices, pushing consumers to the illicit market and forcing small businesses to close because they can’t absorb the costs, leading to industry consolidation that could stifle innovation and reduce competition," as speculated by Michael Brosgart, president of cannabis vaping company Active.

With an end to end US manufacturing yet available while potentially being economically inefficient, it is unsurprising why NLCP and IIPR have been corrected as they have after the 2024 stock price heights, with it remaining to be seen if they are able to weather the intermediate term uncertainties.

So, Is NLCP Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

NLCP 3Y Stock Price

For now, NLCP has already lost -32.7% or the equivalent -$139.61M of its market capitalization from the 52-week heights, otherwise, -53.9% or -$376.04M from the all-time heights, respectively, as the cannabis industry faces mounting headwinds.

On the one hand, readers must note that the meltdown has already triggered its richer forward dividend yields of 11.91%, compared to the 4Y averages of 7.80% and the REIT sector median of 4.36%, with its pay-outs remaining well covered by the ongoing operations, assuming no further tenant issues.

If anything, we must highlight that IIPR has made the decision to maintain its quarterly dividend payout sum in April 2025, despite the recent tenant headwinds and the FQ1'25 AFFO per share decline to $1.94 (-12.6% QoQ/-12.2% YoY) in FQ1'25.

On the other hand, it remains to be seen when the overall cannabis headwinds may end, as discussed in the risk section above, since IIPR's decision to continue paying dividends has already triggered a very risky and higher AFFO payout ratio of 97% (+11 points QoQ/+15 YoY).

This is on top of the higher net debt levels of -$161.48M (-6.5% QoQ/-12.6% YoY), with the REIT likely to face a deteriorating balance sheet if they do not cut dividends and/or its rental collection does not improve.

As a result, while we may cautiously rate the NLCP stock as a speculative Buy here, attributed to the rich dividend investment thesis, it goes without saying that our rating is only suitable for those with higher risk tolerance, since it is uncertain if the REIT's tenants may be able to be an exception to the ongoing rental defaults.

This stock may not be for the faint-hearted indeed.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Mister Car Wash: Various Near-Term Headwinds Limit Upside Potential
data.description:
Investment action

I give a hold rating for Mister Car Wash (MCW) because while MCW continues to execute well operationally, the uncertain macro backdrop and its potential impact on consumer discretionary spending are significant near-term risks that may hurt MCW's near-term earnings outlook. My stance is to wait for more clarity before turning bullish.

Company description

MCW is a car wash chain in the US with around 518 locations as of 1Q25. The main services it provides are express exterior and interior cleaning. It has a membership model called Unlimited Wash Club [UWC] that currently has >2.2 million members. The model charges members a monthly fee in exchange for unlimited washes and dedicated lane access. As of 1Q25, the revenue split between UWC sales and normal retail sales is 73% vs. 27%.

1Q25 Earnings Review

MCW reported 9.4% y/y 1Q25 revenue growth with a solid 6% growth in comparable store sales [CSS]. Demand was steady across both UWC and retail. UWC grew membership by 5% y/y to >2.2 million, and total UWC revenue grew 8.8% y/y. As for retail sales, revenue grew 10.9% y/y. Profitability also moved in the right direction. Adj. EBITDA grew 13.9% to $85.6 million, with margins expanding 130 bps to 32.7%. As a result, net income came in at $35 million, or $0.11 per share. MCW also delevered its balance sheet by voluntarily paying down $62 million in debt during the quarter. The current expectation is for net leverage to go just below 2.5x EBITDA by year-end.

Pricing Power, Good Cost Control, and Expanding Store Base

One of the more important developments for MCW is its first-ever price increase for the base UWC tier. The monthly fee is moving from $19.99 to around $22.99 in most markets, impacting ~40% of the member base. The pace of rollout is expected to be completed by May or June (across 5 regions), as there is a 30–60 day delay to notify members. If this plays out well, this move could meaningfully lift average revenue per member and drive top-line growth. Simple math would mean a ~6% increase to the average selling price for base tier members, or a 6% uplift to UWC revenue directly, assuming no churn. Importantly, management noted that pricing across the industry is becoming more rational, which should continue to give MCW room to raise prices. Complementing MCW’s pricing power is solid cost control, which can be seen from the reduction in labor and chemical costs as a percentage of revenue—down 160 bps—and G&A down 120 bps. In my view, this combination is a strong setup for margin to continue expanding if the churn stays manageable and macro conditions remain stable.

Aside from pricing, MCW also has another growth lever—store expansion—and they have remained aggressive on this front. In 1Q25, MCW opened four new greenfield locations, and management is sticking to their FY25 target of 30 to 35 new stores, implying a sequential ramp-up in the coming quarters. The long-term goal also remains intact—to double the current US footprint organically.

I believe this expansion is plausible because of the easing competitive landscape. MCW noted just 7 new competitor stores opening within a 3-mile radius in 1Q25, down from 15 last year and 33 the year before. This is a stark contrast to MCW's expansion record—opening 33 and 38 in FY24/23, respectively. The way I see it, it shows that the industry is consolidating, and MCW is taking advantage to capture more real estate space. Encouragingly, even in areas where competition does show up, management noted that stores facing new rivals tend to recover and outperform the chain average within 18 to 24 months.

Potential Headwinds

Now, I don’t think this is the right time to invest in MCW is because of a few potential headwinds that could hurt the business in the near term.

Firstly, while the UWC price hike should lift revenue, I think it is better to monitor the churn impact before turning bullish. Despite the encouraging results from test markets, management did acknowledge an “initial increase in churn” from this move. Also, remember that consumers in test markets may not be representative of consumers in other regions. If cancellations come in more than expected—especially in a weaker macro environment—it could offset the benefit of higher pricing and weigh on net membership growth.

Secondly, and this is perhaps the more concerning reason, UWC growth is ultimately tightly linked to retail traffic. Management called out that 5% member growth in 1Q25 tracked almost exactly with a 5% lift in retail visits. That makes the retail channel a key driver—and a major risk as well. If retail traffic softens, subscription growth could slow down as well. And given the macro backdrop, I don’t think this is an unlikely scenario. The US economy has already shown signs of weakness, including a GDP contraction in 1Q25 and persistent inflation pressure. Consumers are clearly feeling it, as indicated by the drop in the consumer confidence index. This is very important because pay-per-wash visits are likely among the first to be cut when consumers tighten their budget. And if those visits decline, it directly shrinks the pool of potential new UWC sign-ups.

Valuation

On the valuation front, I wouldn’t jump to say that MCW is cheap just because it is at the low end of its historical trading band—16x NTM PE. I think the market is right to value it at the current price because things could go both ways. If the US economy holds up better than expected, MCW will have no issues pushing through price increases and staying on its aggressive store expansion strategy. However, if the economy gets worse—consumers further tighten their budget—we could see a sharp drop in car wash demand (consumers can just wash themselves to save cost), and this directly impacts UWC growth. In the latter scenario, earnings growth is likely to be negative, and I do not want to be invested when that happens. As such, I think the current multiple is fair. This does not mean that MCW is uninvestable. I think the right timing to invest is when we see a few things: (1) UWC price increases face no churn; (2) the US economy gradually gets better; and (3) no step up in competitive pressure. All of these should become clearer in the coming quarters.

Conclusion

I am giving a hold rating on MCW for now. The near-term setup is a bit murky, and there is real execution risk around the UWC price increase, especially if churn runs higher than expected. And with retail traffic so tightly linked to subscription growth, any slowdown from a cautious consumer could weigh on growth momentum. Until there’s more clarity on how these factors play out, I think it makes sense to stay on the sidelines.

data.title: Goldman Sachs BDC Trades At 78 Cents On A Dollar, But Is The 'Dollar' Stable?
data.description:
Goldman Sachs BDC (NYSE:GSBD) has had a rough ride over the past year and a half. For example, since my first bearish article on GSBD that was published back in July 2024, the BDC has lost more than one-third of its market cap. As I expected, earlier this year, GSBD announced a dividend cut, which has not so far changed the situation to the positive side. In my Q4, 2024 earnings report review piece, I still outlined a skeptical thesis, arguing that the chances were higher for a further downside than a sudden recovery. Since then, the total return result has been negative, at ~15%.

Now, GSBD has circulated its Q1, 2025 earnings figures, which provide a fresh base of information for us to contextualize with the current dividend yield of ~12.2% and a P/NAV of 0.78x (i.e., discount of 22%).

The entry point in GSBD looks optically very attractive.

The question is whether the dividend is sustainable and whether the existing discount has already baked into the cake all of the known and some part of the currently unknown risks.

Let's try to answer this together by dissecting the most recent data points.

Thesis review

As the title indicates, what we have to really understand is whether the NAV or the “dollar” is stable enough that paying “78 cents” for it would make it a bargain. The sustainability of the dividend yield is related but a secondary aspect. If the NAV can remain stable or even grow without the help of a potentially reduced dividend, then we have a beautiful story here for tangible current income and very likely price appreciation.

Looking at the Q1, 2025 report, my immediate reaction is that GSBD has still failed to stabilize its operations.

In Q1, 2025, the adjusted NII per share decreased to $0.41 from $0.47 per share in the prior quarter. The drop was driven by a significant reduction in the interest income (due to lower portfolio yield) and increasing interest expense.

While the $0.41 per share result was sufficient to cover the base dividend of $0.32 per share, if we consider the supplemental distribution and unrealized losses, the generated adjusted NII was still not enough to avoid the NAV decline. Namely, the NAV per share came in at $13.20—a notable drop relative to the Q4, 2024 statistic of $13.41 per share.

There are two concerns in this context. One is adjusted NII per share related, and the other one is net unrealized/realized loss related.

The first aspect has two sides. The positive is that Q1, 2025, already includes all of the lagged effects from the lower base rates. Plus, the amortized portfolio yield sits at 10%, which is not a significant difference from where the market financing levels are. So, headwinds from the yield compression dynamics are likely to be over or remain manageable.

On the negative side, I have to mention these factors that could drive the figure lower going forward:

  • 2026 fixed unsecured note refinancing that has a yield of just 2.87%. Clearly, the new loan will cost more.
  • Lower asset base from negative net funding volume in Q1, 2025.
  • Incremental non-accruals, which we have to factor into our base case.

However, the base dividend coverage is at 128%, which provides a decent cushion for additional shock absorption.

The second aspect is a bit bigger concern. It is the key reason why the NAV has declined. And looking back at the past 4 financial quarters, we will see that in each quarter there has been a struggle for GSBD to avoid a net negative result from the unrealized/realized gain component. In this quarter, the hit came from the assumption pertaining to the portfolio company's potential challenges that could be induced by the tariffs. It is very hard to predict how this specific component will evolve going forward. The recent history is not on the GSBD investor side. Furthermore, I would say that the portfolio-wide company credit statistics do not send an encouraging signal either:

  • Weighted average interest coverage is 1.9x, which is not bad, but not good either. For example, my favorite BDC – Kayne Anderson (NYSE:KBDC) – has interest coverage above 3x.
  • Weighted average leverage (net debt-to-EBITDA) is 5.8x, which is clearly skewed towards the more aggressive side.

Finally, if we speak about growth, then I do not see an overly positive outlook for GSBD. The debt-to-equity is at 1.16x, which is within a typical BDC sweet spot range. Going above that would be just too aggressive, given the underlying risks and/or exposures towards potentially weak portfolio companies. Plus, the capital/M&A market itself is frozen, which makes growth very difficult.

The bottom line

In a nutshell, I think that GSBD's NAV is far from being stable. Or let me rephrase that. While it is not subject to very steep and even dangerous losses, the odds are still stacked in favor of seeing further NAV reductions, at least over the next couple of quarters. The net unrealized/realized loss momentum, weak portfolio company credit statistics, and unfavorable market environment do not particularly help to maintain the NAV unchanged.

The good thing, however, is that GSBD's dividend coverage of 128% leaves a solid amount of undistributed income in the system that can act as a shield from NAV-negative items.

At the end of the day, we have to decide what level of risk we are willing to take. GSBD is definitely a high-risk play, but with not that bad risk-to-reward ratio. Also, the downside is not totally unprotected.

I would not invest in GSBD for stable total returns. While the dividend looks safe, the NAV is vulnerable and could trigger tangible price declines.

If I decided to invest in GSBD, then it would be a minor position with an expectation that, in case the markets recover, GSBD should lead to outsized alpha performance. However, I am not planning to do so, because of CION Investment Corporation (NYSE:CION), which, in my view, embodies a greater risk-to-reward ratio as a speculative investment.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Boots, Beats & Billion-Dollar Brands
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Pop culture icons like Beyoncé and Taylor Swift are fueling a major resurgence in western wear, from cowboy boots to denim. Their influence, along with shows like Yellowstone and the SEC football scene, has helped push brands like Levi’s (LEVI) and Boot Barn (BOOT) into the spotlight. Despite short-term stock dips, the western wear market is projected to grow from $80B in 2022 to $136B by the end of 2031, showing how culture can drive real economic trends.

This is an abridged transcript.

Even if you aren't a Beyonce, Taylor Swift or even college football fan. You’ve seen the effect each has had on western wear.

Cowboy Carter dropped in true Queen Bey fashion more than a year ago in March 2024 and we’re still talking about it…it’s a Grammy Award winning album of the year…not to mention Best Country Album.

Taylor Swift's Era's Tour started in March of 2023 and ended more than a year later. Concert goers were decked out in friendship bracelets, anything that sparkled and of course cowboy boots.

And if you’ve been to any SEC football game cowboy boots will let everyone know this ain't your first rodeo. And no you don’t know anyone with a pair white cowboy boots with LSU woven on the side.

Not to mention Yellowstone, 1883, and if I'm dating myself, Walker Texas Ranger.

But let's go back to the beginning, Beyonce. Cowboy Carter continues to thrive.

The tour has kicked off and yes the conversation surrounding Beyonce's twang set to music with a whole lotta guitar strumming and whews continues. A search of the internet will show fans blazin’ in head to toe country chic.

Now you’re listening to this thinking, Julie, what does this have to do with the economy or stocks? That’s why I tune in to Seeking Alpha.

Well here we are.

Yes, there has always been a demand for western wear. Who doesn’t love a Canadian or Texas tuxedo?

Ya know the cowboy boots, the wide brim hats, leather belts and well-fitting jeans.

But how much has the industry grown or how much is it projected to grow?

Track 17 on the Cowboy Carter album is simply entitled “Levii’s Jeans.” And of course there’s the commercials with Beyonce strutting around in Levi's. Levi Strauss &Co (LEVI) President and CEO Michelle Gass had this to say on the most recent earnings call in April.

While many brands aspire to be at the center of culture, we are firmly there. You can see that through our partnership with the most influential artist of our generation, Beyonce. Since launching REIIMAGINE in September of 2024, the campaign has generated more than 4.3 billion impressions and more than $65 million in estimated earned media value, continuing to help us drive equity gains with men and women across our markets.

Now we have to point out that LEVI is down 27 percent over the past year so if you’re an investor you may say…the math ain't mathin’. OK. That’s fair but long term over the past 5 years LEVI is up 36%.

By the way, I am not promoting a particular stock. I’m just giving you the facts.

According to Transparency Market Research, before Cowboy Carter was released and before Taylor Swift took to the stage, western wear was an $80B market in 2022 and at that time the firm projected it to grow to 136 billion dollars by the end of 2031.

Let’s look at another stock, Boot Barn Holdings, Inc (BOOT).

The retail store offers western and work-related footwear, apparel and accessories.

BOOT last reported in January and is set to report again later this month.

Boot Barn Holdings, Inc. (BOOT) is expanding its store base and has a commitment of new store growth of 15 percent annually.

On the earnings call a JPMorgan analyst asked management to elaborate on traffic demand trends back in January.

But looking at the specific department in January, we saw an acceleration in both men's and women's Western categories, specifically boots and apparel. So we saw a nice business in those four major merchandise categories. As we look forward to the remainder of the quarter, there's nothing that we're seeing that makes us feel any different about the business. It was a strong start to our fourth quarter, and we think that'll continue.

That was John Hazen, the Interim Chief Executive Officer.

Year-to-date BOOT is down 27%, over the past year it’s up about 5 percent and long term over the past 5 years it’s up 505%.

Tecovas is another brand. No, it’s not publicly traded but a quick look at their website showed cowboy boots for women ranging between $250 and more than $500. Oh the price tag!

The company was started by a native Texan a decade ago. The boots are still designed in Austin and crafted in Mexico and Vietnam. Tecovas says it's the fastest growing western brand in the world.

And finally Ulta (ULTA). I know what you’re thinking isn’t that a beauty store. Yep! It sure is. But if you have the app. Open it. Or just go to the website. On the app you’ll see a pop up that says Ulta (ULTA) is the official beauty partner of the Cowboy Carter tour. The site highlights what you need to buy to have the perfect Cowboy Carter glam not to mention the fact that if you want to buy Cecred in a store…ya know…Beyonce’s hair care brand…it can only be found at Ulta…ticker symbol ULTA.

Brands like MAC which is owned by Estee Lauder (EL), a publicly traded company and Hourglass Cosmetics which is owned by Unilever (UL) and also public are highlights on the list.

But you may be thinking what about tariffs? Are these companies prepared? What is their exposure? We’ll consider two companies mentioned earlier, Levi Strauss (LEVI) and Boot Barn (BOOT).

Here’s what Harmit Singh, the Chief Financial and Growth Officer of Levi Strauss (LEVI) had to say on the conference call on April 7th:

For now, our full year outlook remains unchanged and includes no impact from the proposed tariffs. Our Q2 outlook remains consistent with our internal plan. The impacts of tariffs will have a minimal impact to our margin structure in the quarter as most of the product for spring, early summer is already in the U.S.. As a reminder, Q2 is seasonally our lowest quarter for revenue and margins in the year.

We look forward to an update when LEVI reports earnings on July 1 after the market closes.

BOOT is set to report sooner on May 14th.

Hazen spoke about tariffs on that same call from earlier.

Yes, we're as a reminder we're 30% on order with China, 25% on order with Mexico. China's more rubber-soled performance boots and apparel of course and then Mexico is where we make our leather soled cowboy boots and where the some of the best leather soled cowboy boots or most of the leather-soled cowboy boots are made. We are testing other countries but we're not going to overreact right now. There are great boots made in Mexico and there's supply chain efficiencies we're looking at in terms of how we bring those boots up and how we go about manufacturing those boots and then putting some pressure on our vendors and then we will maintain margin.

So we'll have to pass, if there is a significant tariff, we're going to have to work on getting better pricing, some economies of scale on the supply chain, and perhaps passing some of that price increase onto the customer to maintain our margin profile.

By the way, did you know Seeking Alpha has the transcripts and recordings for earnings calls. So I’ll leave a link in show notes for Ulta’s (ULTA) earnings call back in March.

Earlier I said the forward outlook shows more than a $50B boom for the western wear business in less than 10 years. Will that happen? Only time will tell.

And now when I hear, “These boots are made for walking,“ it just hits different.

data.title: BDC Weekly Review: Bimodal Q1 Numbers Weigh On The Sector
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company [BDC] sector from both the bottom-up–highlighting individual news and events –as well as the top-down – providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the first week of May.

Market Action

BDCs fell around 1.5% on the week, pulled lower by relatively weak sentiment on the back of a couple of bad Q1 reports. The sector has given back some of its earlier rally and is still more than half below its pre-Liberation Day level.

Year-to-date, it is mostly lower-quality or high-valuation BDCs that are down the most.

April marked the second consecutive month with the worst performance since Oct. 2022.

The median valuation in our coverage is well off the historical average and is about the same distance from its recent trough.

Market Themes

As we have discussed a few times recently, BDCs tend to follow stocks day-to-day. This doesn't mean that longer-term BDC returns will mirror that of the equity market, just that the daily moves tend to be in the same direction and roughly similar in magnitude. However, this pattern changed this week with BDCs heading lower while stocks continued to rally.

Part of the reason for this could be a few bad Q1 reports (see below) which created anxiety for the broader sector, driving down prices of BDCs that have not yet reported along with big drops of those with disappointing numbers. Investors may be taking the view that the few bad prints are evidence that the sector will struggle in a recession - a macro outcome that looks increasingly likely.

A handful of bad reports, however, shouldn't be a surprise. For one, we have discussed recently that investors should be expecting a fall in NAVs across the sector, which we are already seeing.

And two, BDCs with bad Q1 results have so far been precisely those that have reported bad numbers before. What we are not seeing yet are historically resilient BDCs reporting unusually bad numbers. In fact, the opposite. Historically resilient BDCs are reporting ROE figures that are near or above double-digit levels, not quite as high as the peak of strong recent markets but more than respectable.

Our view is that a broad-based dip in prices in the sector would mark an attractive time to add an allocation to certain BDCs, just like it was right after Liberation Day. However, the second dip is likely to be more prolonged than the first, requiring more patience from investors.

Market Commentary

Oaktree Specialty Lending (OCSL) reported another tough quarter with a -2.4% total NAV return. NII fell $0.1 to $0.45, prompting the company to slash its supplemental dividend. Non-accruals have risen to 4.6% on fair value and 7.6% on cost. The stock fell nearly 8% after the news, extending its rough ride to -31% over the past year.

All of the losses were due to unrealized appreciation, so arguably they can be made back and OCSL does have a history of sharp writedowns. The company has generated almost no return over the last year vs. a +11% total NAV return for the median BDC in coverage.

Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN) had another shocker with a -6.6% total NAV return for the quarter. It might be tempting to conclude that it was due to spread widening over Q1 and that might be part of it, however, the company has been a slow-motion train wreck for quite some time now. 5 of the last 7 quarters were negative, so investors shouldn’t be surprised at the negative Q1 print, even if the latest result is the worst in the post-COVID period.

All of the loss was in unrealized appreciation, so we can’t rule out some upside reversal down the line. Recall that HRZN announced an agreement to be acquired by Monroe Capital in February 2023. This might be a complete coincidence, but the company’s total NAV return since then is -14% or so. That’s not the worst result in coverage (PSEC and TPVG are down by over 30% in total NAV terms since then) but it’s pretty striking. MRCC which is a Monroe BDC has poor returns as well though not as bad as HRZN.

On the other side of the ledger, we have seen strong results as well. Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX) delivered a strong result of 2.5%. It benefited from a sharp drop in interest expense as all of its debt is floating-rate. Non-accruals were in good shape and fell to 1.2%. There was a net realized gain. Hercules Capital (HTGC) put up a strong result as well with a 3.2% total NAV return though this was below its previous run-rate. Unrealized depreciation caused a 1% drop in the NAV.

Stance and Takeaways

The BDC sector screens as attractive at the moment, however, we are not adding to our holdings at the moment. This is because we topped up our BDC allocation by 5% (in absolute terms in the more actively managed High-Income Portfolio) in the first 3 days of the Liberation Week as markets fell sharply and the median BDC valuation widened to a 20% discount.

The sector has rallied since then, and we already closed out the GAIN position at a double-digit profit with the rest of the new holdings sitting at 5-10% gains. The market has bounced more quickly than we expected and with the stock market having erased all of its April losses, there is much less margin of safety priced in given the challenges on the horizon.

data.title: Market Reversal: REITs Beating Tech In 2025
data.description:
Co-written by Austin Rogers.

Real estate investment trusts ("REITs") have endured a perfect storm of bad news over the last several years.

While most commentators start the narrative about REIT troubles in 2022, when interest rates surged on the back of hot inflation, the underappreciated reality is that the seeds of the perfect storm were planted in 2020 and 2021.

That two-year period in the midst of the pandemic benefited REITs in the short term but also gave property developers their lowest cost of capital in history. Naturally, given cheap debt and a robust economic rebound, developers greenlit a huge number of projects across the country (especially the fast-growing Sunbelt states). By late 2023 and 2024, those projects began to come online, adding huge amounts of new supply at exactly the same time as the post-COVID economic rebound was losing steam. Over the last few years, existing real estate portfolios (including those owned by REITs) have experienced downward pressure on occupancy rates and rent growth.

This mild softening in REIT fundamentals, combined with elevated interest rates, led to major underperformance against the S&P 500 (SPY) and especially the Big Tech stocks in the Nasdaq index (QQQ).

In 2025, however, the headwinds that have pulled down REITs over the last few years are rapidly reversing into positive trends. Property deliveries are falling, and interest rates are drifting back down (though not to the ultra-low levels that led to oversupply).

It's no wonder, then, that REITs are significantly outperforming the market and specifically Big Tech this year.

In what follows, we'll show some charts that illustrate the story above, and then we'll look at a few REITs that remain good buying opportunities.

Market Rotation Into REITs

Going into 2023, REITs were around their average long-term valuations while Big Tech stocks, especially the Magnificent 7 (MAGS), were trading at discounts to their long-term valuations after a tech-driven selloff in 2022.

That set the stage for a two-year period of massive Big Tech outperformance over REITs as the former enjoyed strong earnings growth on the back of AI investments while the latter suffered headwinds from interest rates and supply deliveries.

Above, we use the SPDR Real Estate Sector ETF (XLRE), which holds all S&P 500 REITs, and the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ), which holds all real estate stocks, to measure the performance of REITs.

Most market participants believed that the status quo of 2023-2024 would continue into 2025, and for the first month, it did.

Then a Chinese company released its DeepSeek R1 model, the new administration came in and created significant policy uncertainty, and nominal GDP growth estimates began to decline, bringing interest rates down as well.

In this environment, REITs have increasingly pulled ahead of Big Tech.

Given continued global AI competition, policy uncertainty, and growth estimate downgrades, we think the market will continue to rotate into REITs this year and extend that outperformance over Big Tech.

Commercial Real Estate Has Troughed

What about real estate fundamentals?

Let's start by looking at the commercial real estate value index compiled by the National Council of Real Estate Investment Fiduciaries ("NCREIF"). What we find is that the spike in interest rates combined with the wave of new supply and remote work trend caused the second-largest recorded drop in CRE values going back to 1977.

However, in late 2024 and into 2025, that decline in CRE values has halted, at least for the average property. Certain pockets of CRE are still experiencing pricing weakness, including downtown office towers. But for most sectors of CRE, the worst has passed. Fundamentals are plateauing, setting the stage for a rebound to come later this year and into 2026.

We can see the steady weakening in rent growth, especially in the two hottest sectors coming out of the pandemic (apartments and industrial), from 2022 through today.

But given the reversal in supply coming to market over the course of this year and through 2026, rent growth is expected to trough and start its rebound sometime this year.

The drop in new construction projects cannot be overstated for the bull case for REITs.

Here's what that looks like for apartments:

Given roughly two-year development timelines, the peak level of construction starts in 2022 led to peak deliveries in the second half of 2024. Over the course of this year, there will be fewer and fewer new properties coming to market to compete with existing properties.

Good Opportunities Still Available

Even after the REIT rally of the last year or so, there are still some great buying opportunities in this space.

Where are we most bullish? Frankly, it is hard to pick only a sector or two.

In the bull market following the Great Financial Crisis of 2008-2009, industrial REITs like First Industrial (FR) and EastGroup Properties (EGP) led the way in performance, driven by the growth of e-commerce. Meanwhile, the retail sector lagged as store closures outnumbered store openings for several years.

Over the last few years, however, that situation has reversed. Store openings have outnumbered closures over the last few years, while there has been basically no new retail space delivered, on net.

In fact, the fundamentals for high-quality retail centers look very good for at least the next few years, as high occupancy rates push up rents while landlords see very little competition from new supply.

That leads to the first good buying opportunity: Whitestone REIT (WSR).

WSR owns shopping centers in affluent areas of five fast-growing Sunbelt cities: Phoenix, Houston, Dallas/Fort Worth, San Antonio, and Austin.

One way to discern the location quality of and tenant demand for WSR's centers is by observing how much same-store NOI growth it has achieved compared to capex spending.

Many of WSR's shopping center peers have to pay significant sums of capital for redevelopment projects or tenant buildouts in order to increase rent rates and NOI.

Moreover, the primary concern the market has had with WSR over the past few years -- its debt -- is gradually fading as the company deleverages.

Note that debt-to-EBITDA has plunged by over three points since the beginning of 2021, resulting from both debt paydown and EBITDA growth.

No debt matures this year, and next year, the only maturity is WSR's floating-rate credit revolver.

WSR currently trades at a price to FFO (funds from operations) multiple of about 11.5x. That may not be dirt cheap, but given the quality of the REIT's portfolio and its above-average growth rate, we think it could have another ~25% upside to a 15x multiple. On top of that, management expects to grow earnings by 5-7% per year going forward, and the REIT pays a monthly dividend currently yielding 4.3%.

Altogether, that gives WSR a total return profile in the high single-digit or low-double-digit territory.

Another attractive opportunity still present in today's market is BSR REIT (OTCPK:BSRTF), a Sunbelt Class B multifamily REIT with a portfolio heavily concentrated in the Texas Triangle of Houston, Dallas/Fort Worth, and Austin.

We have been arguing for a while that BSR's fundamental value is being significantly underpriced, largely due to the fact that it is primarily listed on the Toronto Stock Exchange instead of an American exchange.

That undervaluation was recently proven by hard data, as AvalonBay Communities (AVB) purchased 9 properties (~2,700 units) from BSR at a purchase price of $618.5 million, representing a fairly low average cap rate of 5.1%.

Like WSR, BSR has enjoyed best-in-class earnings growth over the last few years, driven overwhelmingly by organic NOI growth.

Likewise, the REIT's net asset value ("NAV") per share growth has surpassed all but one of its Sunbelt peers:

Even so, BSR continues to trade at a significant discount to its Sunbelt multifamily REIT peer group:

At the current share price, BSR offers a well-covered (even after the portfolio sale), monthly paid dividend yielding 4.6%.

We think the REIT also has at least 25% upside to fair value, and while you wait for the REIT's shareholder-aligned management team to realize that value, you can enjoy that highest-in-class dividend yield.

Bottom Line

It has been tough ride for REIT investors over the last several years. Many investors thought it was lunacy to keep buying beaten down REITs through their bear market. But when solid companies are beaten down is exactly when it is best to buy them!

Today, members of High Yield Landlord are enjoying the fruits of having continued to invest right through the REIT bear market, and now we are looking forward to a robust REIT bull market.

Some great buying opportunities still exist, and you can be sure that we are constantly on the hunt for more of them.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Invest In 6 Funds For 6% Income With SWAN-Like Features
data.description:
Introduction:

The market has been recovering for the past couple of weeks after throwing some tariff tantrums. It appears that the worst for the market may be over, though it can change on a dime. However, there are some positive catalysts. First, President Trump's tariff threats appear as more of a negotiating tactic and being able to make some favorable deals than the actual long-term impacts. Now, a lot of folks will disagree with the method and its madness. But we have seen time and again that the worst-case scenarios are diluted and watered down quickly. For instance, take the case of auto-related tariffs. The administration announced a blanket 25% tariff on both finished autos and auto parts, but then it was paused a couple of times. This was followed by some serious lobbying by many of the top executives from the auto industry with the administration, resulting in further concessions being announced spanning at least the first two years. Further, the products and parts covered under the USMCA (US, Mexico, and Canada) trade agreement are already exempt. Sure, there will still be some tariffs and disincentives for the imports, but it may not be as bad or far-reaching as it appeared to start with. Similar is the case with other nation-specific reciprocal tariffs. It's likely that many important trading countries will wrap up some kind of deal or understanding prior to the expiry of the 90-day pause. Sure, China is still the elephant in the room, and it remains a problem. But both the U.S. and China are working out quietly and granting exemptions where it really hurts. So, the trend is clear, and the market appears to have got a handle on this. This is the biggest reason that the market has slowly recovered to the level prior to the announcement of tariffs.

Secondly, there are other measures and factors that some suggest could favor investor sentiment. Some of them include the progress on the tax cut bill in Congress, likely some action by the Fed in June (if not in May), as well as positive long-term impacts from deregulation and shrinking of the federal government. The Trump administration is also proposing to provide 100% depreciation expenses for investments in plant, machinery, and equipment that would allow businesses to deduct investment expenses, especially in manufacturing activity. If this passes through Congress, some say it could provide a boost to economic and manufacturing activity.

Though nothing can be said with any degree of certainty, we think it may be the right time to invest or consider long-term investments. Where you invest should obviously be guided by your own goals, investment horizon, and personal factors, but we will present one investment thesis for income-focused investors who also want reasonable growth while protecting their capital. We believe this aligns very well with most retirees or otherwise conservative income investors.

Investment Goals:

Briefly, the following are the goals and objectives for today's portfolio:

  1. The portfolio should provide roughly 6% income.
  2. The portfolio should provide market-matching total returns over the long term.
  3. The portfolio should provide some level of protection (capital conservation) in an environment of economic upheavals or other challenges. The goal is 20% less volatility and drawdowns.
  4. The portfolio should be easy to implement and maintain with low expenses.

The Portfolio of Six Funds:

Table-1: Portfolio of Six funds

Chart-1: Portfolio allocation

Note(s):

  1. The fund JHQAX (A series) is a mutual fund. JPMorgan also offers a similar ETF with a similar strategy, named Jpmorgan Hedged Equity Laddered Overlay ETF (HELO). JHQAX and HELO have some subtle differences, but they both have similar features.
  2. FNV is a Gold Royalty company. There are alternatives that are similar, such as Wheaton Precious Metals Corp. (WPM). One could also divide the capital allocated to gold into these two or more royalty companies (or gold-specific ETFs).

Sector Allocation:

Chart-1A: Sector-wise allocation

Analysis of Risk vs Returns:

Here's a rough analysis of returns vs. risk based on a relatively small period from January 2023 to April 30, 2025.

Table-1B: Analysis of Risk vs. Returns

Ticker

(SCHD)

(JHQAX)

Backtested Performance:

It's quite difficult to backtest this portfolio for the simple reason that many of the securities have rather short histories. Even a popular dividend ETF like SCHD only goes as far back as 2012. The JEPQ fund has less than three years of history, but it's easy to replace it with a similar fund as there are many. However, on the other hand, a fund like JAAA does not have similar or equivalent funds that may also have more than 10 years of history. In fact, they all started in the last about five years or so. Nonetheless, for the backtesting purpose, we replaced JAAA with a mutual fund, the Fidelity Capital & Income Fund (FAGIX), which provides a very similar performance as JAAA but with much higher volatility than JAAA.

So, here's the performance chart for the period of January 2014 to April 30, 2025.

Table-1C: Backtested Results

Period: Jan. 2014 to Apr. 30, 2025

Fund-Based Model

S&P 500

Annualized Performance - CAGR

12.27%

12.09%

Standard Deviation (volatility)

13.0%

14.8%

Maximum Drawdown

-20.3%

-23.9%

Best Year

33.0%

31.3%

Worst Year

-13.5%

-18.2%

Detailed Information on Funds:

(SCHD) Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF:

SCHD is a highly popular dividend ETF among dividend and income investors. The twin reasons for its popularity come from the fact that its past record of dividend growth has been impressive, in addition to providing an above-average yield of roughly 4%. The fund holds nearly 100 large-cap and blue-chip companies that also happen to pay decent dividends. The fund is not weighted to the capitalization of its constituents, and that helps it have a much more balanced portfolio of stocks, unlike the S&P 500. The fund is reconstructed every year based on its rules.

Some of the top holdings of SCHD are ConocoPhillips (COP), Verizon (VZ), Altria (MO), Lockheed Martin (LMT), Home Depot (HD), AbbVie (ABBV), PepsiCo (PEP), Cisco (CSCO), Chevron (CVX), and Merck & Co. (MRK).

(ADX) Adams Diversified Equity Fund:

ADX is a closed-end fund that was incepted in October 1929. The fund's main objectives are providing a reasonably high income, paid on a quarterly basis, seeking long-term capital appreciation, and preservation of capital. Its core philosophy is to invest in a broadly diversified, sector-neutral, large-cap equity portfolio. The fund uses zero leverage and currently provides close to a 10% yield.

If you would like more information on this fund, we recently published an article on ADX; here is the link.

(JEPQ) JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF:

As stated earlier, JEPQ has a short history of less than three years, but it's impressive so far. Not only does it provide a very high income on a rather consistent basis, but the capital growth has been good as well.

JEPQ is, in fact, a proxy for Nasdaq-100 stocks, but it also writes call options on up to 20% of its assets using ELNs (Equity Linked Notes). With regard to the call options, they come with a twist: JEPQ uses ELNs instead of plain vanilla call options. The use of options not only generates high income for the fund but also provides some level of protection during a highly volatile period, such as the one we are witnessing currently.

There's a wide choice of similar funds in the CEF arena. Some of the funds amongst the CEFs are Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income Fund II (EOS), Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Diversified Equity Income Fund (ETY), Columbia Seligman Premium Technology Growth Fund (STK), and Nuveen NASDAQ 100 Dynamic Overwrite Fund (QQQX). One could also look at Amplify CWP Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (DIVO).

As expected, JEPQ is filled with large technology names and is very similar to the Nasdaq-100 constituents. Some of its top holdings are Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), NVIDIA (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), Broadcom (AVGO), Meta Platforms (META), and Netflix (NFLX).

Note: The ELNs are derivative instruments that are designed to offer the economic benefits/loss of the covered calls written on the respective indexes.

(JHQAX) JPMorgan Hedged Equity Fund:

JHQAX is a mutual fund from the JPMorgan group. This fund provides broad equity exposure with an element of hedging. Equity exposure provides decent growth over the years, while hedging provides protection against corrections and severe downturns.

The fund broadly invests in a portfolio of U.S. large-cap stocks. It then employs a disciplined options strategy that seeks to reduce downside risk in falling markets.

For folks who may not like a mutual fund and rather prefer an ETF, JPMorgan provides an equivalent ETF under the same management team that is quite similar but has subtle differences and a short history. In some ways, HELO may be a better choice, as it uses a laddered options strategy to provide protection. The ETF version is Jpmorgan Hedged Equity Laddered Overlay ETF (HELO).

One could also look at iShares U.S. Equity Factor Rotation Active ETF (DYNF) offered by the BlackRock group, which offers an alternative strategy. The underlying strategies of DYNF and JHQAX are quite different. The only thing common is that both aim for lower volatility than the broader market. However, DYNF did not achieve lower volatility but provided much better returns than JHQAX.

Below, we provide the comparative action (JHQAX vs. SPY) during the tariff tantrum period from March to April 30th. You can see the difference in volatility.

Chart-2:

(JAAA) Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF:

JAAA invests in AAA-rated CLO (Collateralized Loan Obligations) debt investments. What makes it a highly defensive fund is the fact that 100% of its assets are in AAA-rated debt with minimal risk of defaults. This is considered a defensive fixed-income asset class, especially during uncertain times (as apparent from the chart presented below). The AAA-rated CLO investments of this fund should not be confused with the broader CLO market, which has varied levels of rated tranches. For example, a fund that invests in BBB-rated CLOs will be much riskier. The fund is also highly diversified into 400-plus individual securities spanning many sectors. This fund can provide consistent risk-adjusted returns with a low correlation to traditional fixed-income assets like bonds. Since the fund's assets are mostly floating-rate, the high interest rates in recent years have been beneficial to the fund. Unless interest rates go down to zero in a hurry again, the fund is likely to keep performing well and keep providing a high level of income. However, as interest rates come down in the next two years, the income generated by the fund, thereby the yield, will come under pressure.

All that said, this fund is not an alternative to equities, as it will not provide much growth compared to equities. That's why we have a limited 15% allocation to this fund in a portfolio with nearly 75% equity exposure.

Chart-3: One Month Total Returns, JAAA vs. the S&P 500

(FNV) Franco-Nevada Corporation:

FNV is an individual stock representing the gold and precious metals asset class to provide asset-based diversification within the portfolio. FNV is a gold-focused royalty and streaming company. It invests in a diversified portfolio of gold-related cash-producing assets. Essentially, companies like FNV provide a combination of opportunities for gold price appreciation and mining and exploration optionality while limiting cost inflation exposure. As a royalty and streaming company, it provides low-cost funding to potential exploration and new mine build operations in lieu of rights to a portion of their future production. Currently, FNV's portfolio includes roughly 430 assets, out of which nearly 160 are either producing assets or nearing production, while the rest are in exploration stages. Also, 85% of its assets are located in the U.S., Canada, Mexico, and Central/South America.

Another such royalty company to consider would be Wheaton Precious Metals Corp., which has a similar dividend yield.

Alternatively, you could choose to buy a gold-related ETF, such as SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) or SPDR Gold MiniShares ETF (GLDM), but they do not provide any income and charge some fees. We are including FNV as it will also provide some income, even though the yield is quite low at 1% due to the recent appreciation in gold prices and gold-related stocks.

Risks:

The portfolio is highly diversified and reasonably conservative, but it's not without risks.

  1. Market risk: This portfolio has a majority of its investments in equities. So, its risks are highly correlated to the broader market, as represented by the S&P 500. If the broader market were to trend downwards or to have some sort of correction, this portfolio of funds would lose value as well. That said, we expect this portfolio to have lower volatility and lower drawdowns.
  2. Geopolitical risks: The world is highly interconnected, and there are always going to be risks associated with any geopolitical fallouts or unforeseen events.
  3. Risk of economic recession: There are fears of an economic slowdown in the U.S. as well as the rest of the world, especially in light of any prolonged trade tensions.
  4. Slower growth due to hedging: We have 30-40% of the portfolio that utilizes some form of hedging or fixed-income assets. This is great to afford some level of protection, but it also limits the growth prospects in an outright bull market. Moreover, we have 10% exposure to gold-related assets. The gold prices have appreciated a lot during the last 10 years and are expected to continue the upward trend. However, there is always the risk of the trend reversing.

Concluding Thoughts:

We regularly write about how to structure balanced portfolios, including both funds and individual stocks. A portfolio of a limited number of funds offers certain unique advantages. First, they're very easy to implement and highly diversified. They're particularly suited to retirees and investors who like a hands-off approach to investing. Obviously, retirees would like to have a decent level of income as well, and that's why we combine funds that provide high income with others that tend to provide good growth. We also include a couple of funds that aim to provide hedging and fixed income, resulting in a highly balanced and SWAN-like portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
